question: [File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply multiple `token.id=eq:X` query parameters to force direct string interpolation of unparameterized values into the SQL IN clause (`` `in (${tokenIdInParams})` ``), achieving SQL injection that corrupts or drops transaction/token rows, preventing gossip of those transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a `token.id=eq:` value containing SQL metacharacters (e.g., `0.0.1); DROP TABLE token_account;--`) that bypasses EntityId validation and is interpolated raw into the IN clause, causing permanent data loss that prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with hundreds of `token.id=eq:X` parameters, causing the IN clause to grow unboundedly and trigger a catastrophically slow full-table scan on `token_account`, exhausting DB connection pool and blocking all transaction gossip queries?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user submit `token.id=ne:X` to trigger the `InvalidArgumentError` throw path, and by doing so at high volume cause repeated error-handling overhead that degrades the REST API's ability to serve transaction gossip-related endpoints?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an EVM address as `idOrAliasOrEvmAddress` that resolves via `EntityService.getEncodedId()` to trigger a DB lookup on every request, and by flooding with unique EVM addresses exhaust DB connections and prevent transaction gossip processing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a valid account alias string as `idOrAliasOrEvmAddress` to force `EntityService.getAccountIdFromAlias()` DB lookups at high request rate, causing sustained DB load that starves transaction gossip queries of resources?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a non-existent but syntactically valid account ID to force `EntityService.isValidAccount()` to execute a DB query that returns no rows, and by doing so at scale exhaust the DB connection pool and prevent transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user set `limit` to the maximum allowed value and combine it with many `token.id=eq:X` filters to force the largest possible result set, consuming maximum memory and CPU per request and degrading gossip transaction throughput?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user flood requests with unique, valid-but-uncached token IDs to continuously bypass the quickLru cache and force repeated `tokenCacheQuery` DB executions, exhausting DB resources and preventing transaction gossip queries from completing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getCachedTokens()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fixed `maxSize` of the quickLru token cache by cycling through more unique token IDs than the cache capacity, causing constant cache eviction and re-fetching that degrades DB performance and blocks transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger `TokenService.getTokenAccounts()` with a query that returns the maximum number of rows, causing the `rows.reduce()` and `rows.map()` operations to consume excessive CPU/memory and delay gossip transaction responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests for the same valid account ID with maximum limit, causing multiple simultaneous large DB queries that together exhaust the connection pool and prevent transaction gossip from being served?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply both `token.id=gt:X` and `token.id=lt:Y` range conditions simultaneously to produce a complex compound WHERE clause that forces a sequential scan on `token_account`, degrading DB performance and blocking transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a crafted EVM address (e.g., all-zeros or boundary values) that causes `EntityService.getEntityIdFromEvmAddress()` to return multiple rows, triggering an unhandled error state that crashes the handler and prevents transaction gossip responses?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getPaginationLink()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `next` pagination link by injecting crafted query parameters into the request that get reflected into `getPaginationLink()`, causing the next link to point to an attacker-controlled resource and disrupting client transaction gossip flows?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id` value at the maximum long boundary (9223372036854775807) to cause integer overflow or unexpected behavior in `EntityId.parseString()`, producing a malformed SQL query that corrupts transaction data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getInClauseSubQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id=eq:0.0.1` value where the encoded BigInt is directly interpolated into the SQL IN clause without quoting, and by supplying a value like `0.0.1,0.0.2` (if shard/realm parsing is lenient) achieve SQL injection that alters transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply duplicate `token.id=eq:X` parameters for the same token ID, causing the `inConditions` array to grow with duplicates that are all interpolated into the IN clause, producing redundant DB work that degrades gossip transaction throughput?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an alias string that matches multiple alive entities (triggering `multipleAliasMatch` error path in `EntityService`), causing an unhandled server error that crashes the process and prevents transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a very long alias string (e.g., thousands of characters) as `idOrAliasOrEvmAddress` that passes `AccountAlias.isValid()` but causes excessive memory allocation during DB query parameter binding, degrading gossip transaction performance?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenAccounts()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where `getCachedTokens()` is called with a large `Set` of token IDs, causing the `uncachedTokenIds` array to be passed as a single array parameter to `tokenCacheQuery` with `$1 = any ($1)`, and by supplying thousands of IDs cause a DB timeout that blocks transaction gossip?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user send requests with `order=asc` and `order=desc` alternating at high frequency to force repeated re-sorting of large result sets in the DB, causing sustained CPU load that prevents transaction gossip queries from completing?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: extractTokensRelationshipQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an unrecognized filter key that falls into the `default: break` case of the switch statement, causing the filter to be silently ignored and allowing unexpected query shapes that bypass intended access controls on token relationship data?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `token.id` with `EvmAddressType.NUM_ALIAS` format (long-zero EVM address) that is accepted by `filterValidityChecks` but causes unexpected behavior in `getInClauseSubQuery()` when interpolated, leading to malformed SQL that corrupts transaction state?

[File: 'File Name: rest/controllers/tokenController.js -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: getTokenRelationships()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `buildAndValidateFilters()` accepts `acceptedTokenParameters` (only LIMIT, ORDER, TOKEN_ID) to send requests with only `limit=1` and a valid account, causing the server to execute a DB query for every single request at maximum concurrency, exhausting DB connections and blocking transaction gossip?
